steps.title: Identify the structure and signal
steps.content: The sentence expresses two connected ideas: (1) Hopper's career involved more than just solving equations, and (2) as a pioneering programmer, she helped usher in the digital age. These ideas stand in contrast—though her work seemed narrow (equations), it was actually far broader and more transformative. We need punctuation that properly expresses and separates these two parts.

steps.title: Apply the boundary punctuation rule
steps.content:
A colon after a transition word like though introduces an elaboration or contrasting independent clause. In formal writing, this pattern—independent clause, though: [elaboration]—correctly sets up what follows as an explanation of the contrast. This matches choice A: equations, though: introduces the contrasting idea about her role as a pioneering programmer. The other options either mishandle the punctuation (commas around though create awkward parenthetical placement, a period fragments the thought, or no punctuation leaves ambiguous boundaries).

That matches choice A — A is correct.
